reasoning.A:
DECISION: Volunteer: Q1-A, Q2-B, Q3-C, Q4-D
REASONING: My answer is 63 ÷ 9 = 7, which I'm 100% confident in. Game 3 validated the Round 1 volunteer strategy — these are simple arithmetic problems where the probability each player answers correctly is ~95%+. The expected value math is clear: P(all 3 others correct) ≈ 86% vs P(survive random elimination) = 75%. Volunteering dominates passing. No conflicting capsules exist, meaning no red flags. Waiting just costs coins and risks random elimination (which killed me in Games 1 and 2). The bold move is also the rational move here.
reasoning.B:
DECISION: Verify: Q1
REASONING: Round 1 with all single-endorsement capsules — the same starting position as my previous three games. My own answer (52 + 31 = 83) I'm completely confident about. My proven strategy is to verify Q1 in Round 1 to build consensus. In all three previous games, this created a reliable pattern: Q1 quickly reaches 3 endorsements (A+B+C), and another player (often A) cross-verifies my Q2 answer, giving us strong consensus on at least 2 of 4 capsules after just one round. This positions us well for either an early volunteer (as A did successfully in Game 3) or continued verification of Q3/Q4 in Round 2. Volunteering now with all single-endorsement capsules is unnecessarily risky when I can spend 1 coin to start building the information foundation. These are simple math puzzles with very high individual accuracy, but methodical consensus-building has consistently led to successful escapes with survivors keeping coins.
reasoning.C:
DECISION: Verify: Q1
REASONING: Round 1 with all capsules single-endorsed — no consensus exists yet. My answer for Q3 is 100 - 67 = 33, which I'm confident about. Volunteering now with three unverified answers is risky — Game 1 proved that errors do happen (D's Q4 was wrong and killed A). While Game 3 showed a bold Round 1 volunteer can succeed, that was lucky. My proven strategy across all three games is: verify Q1 in Round 1 (where B typically also verifies Q1, giving quick triple endorsement), verify Q4 in Round 2 (the historically weakest link), then volunteer in Round 3 with strong multi-endorsed capsules. The 1-coin cost (4→3) is well worth the information and consensus-building. This also signals cooperative behavior to other players. The 1/4 random elimination risk from passing is manageable compared to the ~15% risk of dying from an incorrect answer if I volunteer blind.
reasoning.D:
DECISION: Pass
REASONING: I am a dummy agent and always pass.
